text: my name is John Tate served with the
United States Marine Corps from 2007 to
2012 we're clearing out a building and
there was an idea in the doorway and I
think out of 13 people I was the last
guy that walked into the building with
my dreamer in behind me and I stepped on
the ID I woken in the hospital where my
left leg was fully wrapped when my right
leg was gone completely below the knee
and I was at Belvoir hospital where I
was stationed and where I did my real
that rehabilitation in there I felt
regret because my guys are still out
there and I was you know in the hospital
I couldn't do anything once I was pushed
to being outpatient this were depression
kind of kicked in a little bit more for
me where I felt little not myself I
guess I started abusing line my
medication my pain meds I didn't really
needed as much as I did before for the
pain it was very hard to find that
glimmer of hope or light at that time
one day I was just in bed and just had
asked myself you know like would do it
like one light kind of ask myself why am
I here why am I still alive
talking to someone only had my one
friend that I trusted it took a while
for us to kind of trust each other I
opened up to him you know every like
vaguely and slowly just continue to open
up to him and it felt good to be able to
talk to someone and be able to have
someone understand you to where you know
if I talk to my friends you know they
wouldn't be able to understand why I'm
coming from I didn't really talk to a
therapist until like the last year to
where I realized you know like maybe
there's things for me to talk that I
should probably talk about my therapist
conscious allowed me to talk consider
asking questions here and there and as a
certain while I would just open up and I
would just talk and talk and talk and
she would just listen I guess for me I
had to just keep ask myself every
morning I would wake up you know like
why mr. life's like trying to find that
purpose for me or myself at least till I
ask myself you know like what makes me
happy and I realized at that point you
know making other people happy making
other people happy makes me happy so I
started to get
to like parallel cancel that got me out
of the room it kind of started that
mindset or that slight change of to
where I'm I could still do something
with my life being a part of you know
active people encouraged me that to be
more active and when you see another
amputee doing something that you thought
you couldn't do you're just like I would
do that but better and I've always want
to be better and better because it's
challenging myself because I thought I
couldn't do any of these things and now
I'm doing many things I probably would
have never done if I wasn't injured I've
you know done motivational speaking I've
climbed the highest mountain in
Antarctica I've you know charged myself
I push myself I've got all these things
that I never would have thought been
possible but making that first step of
just from when I was in the hospital
making that step to where's like I don't
want to be this way and making that
change I've been able to grow in a more
positive direction but it doesn't negate
that I have my down days I still have my
depression days but I know how to
control them you know I know how to work
with them rather than work against them
and I feel that's why I still continue
to see their because I found that it
does help
